items.q: Is a reproducible result the same as a repeatable one?
items.correct: No — repeatable means the same experimenter and method agree; reproducible means others, with different methods or labs, agree.
items.misconception: Yes — the two words are interchangeable.
items.random_wrong: No — reproducible just means it was published twice.
items.why: The terms sound alike, but the distinction matters when judging the strength of experimental evidence.

items.q: Does using a precise instrument guarantee a valid experiment?
items.correct: No — validity depends on controlling variables so the experiment tests the intended relationship.
items.misconception: Yes — better equipment automatically makes the conclusion valid.
items.random_wrong: No — validity only applies to surveys.
items.why: Students focus on equipment quality, but an uncontrolled variable invalidates even the most precise data.

items.q: Can new evidence overturn a well-established theory?
items.correct: Yes — scientific knowledge is provisional and is revised when reliable conflicting evidence appears.
items.misconception: No — once proven, theories become permanent facts.
items.random_wrong: Yes — but only after a hundred years have passed.
items.why: Students see science as a fixed body of facts rather than a self-correcting process driven by evidence.

items.q: How many metres are there in 3.5 km?
items.why: Correct answer: 3500. “350” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The SI base unit of mass is the:
items.why: It's kilogram. “gram” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Independent experts evaluating research before publication.
items.random_wrong: Repeating an experiment until it works.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Independent experts evaluating research before publication.. “The public voting on results.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: The same experimenter gets consistent values using the same method and apparatus.
items.misconception: It was measured only once, very carefully.
items.random_wrong: A different lab gets the same value using different methods.
items.why: Correct answer: The same experimenter gets consistent values using the same method and apparatus.. “It was measured only once, very carefully.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 0.25 A. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
